initial_posts.narrative: As a master's student in chemical engineering at a prestigious university in Seoul, I am part of an education research group supported by BK21. Based on the policy context, the total budget for Hanyang University's BK21 program in 2022 is part of the national allocation. The resource logic states the total support budget for 577 education research groups is 408,080 million KRW, plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support and 2,550 million KRW for evaluation management, totaling 410,597 million KRW. For Hanyang University specifically, I estimate the total funding allocated is around 410.6 billion KRW, as the policy context does not break down by university but gives the national total. For graduate scholarship slots, the policy mentions about 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, totaling 19,000 slots nationally. Hanyang likely has a proportional share, but I estimate the national figure as the prediction field likely refers to the program-wide allocation. For postdoc positions, the policy does not specify a number, but given the minimum salary of 3 million KRW per month and the budget for education research groups (355,151 million KRW), I estimate roughly 300 postdoc positions nationally, assuming each postdoc costs about 36 million KRW per year and a portion of the budget goes to postdocs.
evidence: The policy context states the total support budget for 577 education research groups is 408,080 million KRW, plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support and 2,550 million KRW for evaluation management, totaling 410,597 million KRW for 2022.
The resource logic indicates about 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovative talent training, summing to 19,000 graduate scholarship slots nationally.

The minimum postdoc salary is 3 million KRW per month, and with a total education research group budget of 355,151 million KRW, assuming 10% for postdocs yields about 35515 million KRW, supporting roughly 300 postdocs at 36 million KRW per year each.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoctoral researcher at GIST, I see the activities phase as the operational engine of BK21. The total funds disbursed should match the annual budget of 408,080 million KRW for education research groups, as the two-installment schedule (70% in March, 30% in September) is a standard disbursement mechanism that the NRF follows reliably. Evaluation panels are convened for annual, mid-term, and compliance purposes; I estimate 3 formal meetings per year based on the policy's mention of annual evaluation, mid-term evaluation, and compliance checks. Consulting sessions are offered to groups that request them—the policy states 280 out of 578 groups wished for consulting, so I use that figure. Compliance checks target the 205 innovative talent training groups, as the policy explicitly states those groups are subject to implementation checks. Budget adjustments and reallocations are handled by the Project Oversight Committee based on evaluation outcomes, but that doesn't change the initial disbursement amount. My estimates are grounded in the policy context and my experience with how such programs operate at GIST.
evidence: The policy states the annual budget for education research groups is 408,080 million KRW, and the two-installment schedule (70% March, 30% September) is a standard mechanism that ensures full disbursement by year-end.
The policy mentions annual evaluation, mid-term evaluation, and compliance checks as distinct evaluation activities, each requiring a formal panel meeting, totaling 3 per year.
The policy explicitly states that consulting was conducted for 280 education research groups out of 578 that wished to receive consulting, so I adopt that number.
The policy specifies that 205 education research groups in the innovative talent training project are subject to compliance checks on implementation, so I use that as the count.
